head: stehen /ˈʃteːən/
gloss: To stand; be located or positioned.
notes: • In word-order rules, stehen means a word “stands” or “goes” in a certain sentence position.
• Das Verb steht auf Position 1 means the verb comes first; Position 2 means it comes second.

examples.german: In Ja-/Nein-Fragen steht das Verb auf Position 1.
examples.english: In yes/no questions, the verb comes first.

examples.german: In Antworten und W-Fragen steht das Verb auf Position 2.
examples.english: In answers and W-questions, the verb comes second.
A new car is parked there.

head: die Regel /ˈʁeːɡl̩/
notes: • die Regeln is plural accusative here: the rules being completed.

head: schreiben /ˈʃʁaɪ̯bən/
notes: • Formal command: Schreiben Sie ... = Write ...; verb first, Sie capitalized.

examples.german: Schreiben Sie das in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Write that in the table. [formal you]

head: der Satz /zats/
notes: • die Sätze is plural accusative here: the sentences being written.
• Correct spelling: Sätze with ä, not Satze.

examples.german: Lesen Sie die Sätze noch einmal.
examples.english: Read the sentences one more time. [formal you]

head: Länder, Sprachen, Nationalitäten /ˈlɛndɐ ˈʃpʁaːxən natsi̯onaliˈtɛːtən/
notes: • Nationality nouns are capitalized in German: der Schweizer, die Schweizerin.
• Textbook shorthand Spanier/in means der Spanier / die Spanierin.

examples.german: Sie kommt aus Frankreich und spricht Französisch.
examples.english: She comes from France and speaks French.

examples.german: Er ist Schweizer, sie ist Schweizerin.
examples.english: He is Swiss; she is Swiss.

examples.german: Er ist Spanier, sie ist Spanierin.
examples.english: He is Spanish; she is Spanish.

head: Viel Spaß! /fiːl ʃpaːs/
notes: • Correct spelling in standard German: Spaß with ß, not spas.

head: der Spaß /ʃpaːs/
notes: • Long a + sharp s: Spaß. In Switzerland, ß is usually written ss: Spass.

examples.english: That is a lot of fun.

head: viel /fiːl/
gloss: Much; a lot; many before plural nouns.
notes: • In Viel Spaß!, viel intensifies the wish: literally much fun, naturally have fun.

examples.english: I have a lot of time.

head: zusammen /tsuˈzamən/
notes: • Spelling: zwei m und -en at the end — not zusamen (common typo/OCR).
• In Hoi zusammen!, zusammen means “everyone / all of you” — like English “Hi all!”

head: Hoi /hɔɪ/
gloss: Informal hi or hey; especially typical in Switzerland.
notes: • One-word greeting (interjection), like Hallo or Servus — no article, no plural, no verb endings.
• Very common in Swiss German contexts; also heard in casual speech elsewhere; not the standard classroom default.

head: die Argentinierin /aʁɡɛnˈtsiːnəʁɪn/
gloss: An Argentine; feminine die Argentinierin, masculine der Argentinier.
notes: • Same pattern as die Schweizerin (from die Schweiz): demonyms from Argentinien, the country name in German.

examples.german: Er ist Schweizer, sie ist Schweizerin; er ist Argentinier, sie ist Argentinierin.
examples.english: He is Swiss, she is Swiss; he is Argentine, she is Argentine.

examples.german: Sie ist Argentinierin und lebt in Berlin.
examples.english: She is Argentine and lives in Berlin.

examples.german: Zwei Argentinierinnen und ein Argentinier arbeiten hier.
examples.english: Two Argentine women and one Argentine man work here.

head: Servus /ˈzɛɐ̯vus/
gloss: Informal hi or bye; strongest in Austria and southern Germany.
notes: • Informal greeting and goodbye; most typical in Austria, Bavaria, and other southern regions.
• From Latin for “your servant”; today it is a casual hi, hey, or bye in one short word.
• Not the everyday norm everywhere in Germany; in the north people more often say Hallo or Tschüss.

head: in /ɪn/
gloss: In; inside; on a team (+ dative for location).
notes: • Wo? uses dative: in einem Team. Wohin? uses accusative: in ein Team.

examples.english: You all are on a team.
You all are joining / going into a team. [accusative: direction]

head: der neue Praktikant /deːɐ̯ ˈnɔʏə pʁaktiˈkant/
gloss: The new intern; pattern for adjective endings after der.
notes: • neu becomes neue because it stands before a masculine nominative noun with der.
• After der/die/das, use -e in nominative singular; use -en in most other definite-article forms.

examples.english: The intern is new. [after sein: no adjective ending]
I see the new intern. [accusative masculine: den neuen]

head: auch /aʊ̯x/
notes: • Often comes near what it adds: Ich auch = me too; auch ich = I too / even I.

head: Wie geht’s? /viː ɡeːts/
gloss: How’s it going? / How are you?
notes: • Short for: Wie geht es? Full informal form: Wie geht es dir?
• Common response: Und dir? (= Und wie geht es dir?)
• Another common response: Mir geht’s gut. (= I’m doing well.)
• Formally: Wie geht es Ihnen?

examples.english: How’s it going? / How are you?

head: Freut mich /fʁɔʏ̯t mɪç/
gloss: Nice to meet you; pleased to meet you.
notes: • Short for Es freut mich: literally, it pleases me.
• mich is a personal pronoun in the accusative here, not reflexive; the hidden subject is es.
• Contrast: Ich freue mich means I am happy / pleased; there mich is reflexive because ich and mich refer to the same person.

examples.english: Nice to meet you. [personal-pronoun mich, accusative]

examples.german: Guten Tag, ich heiße Ali. Freut mich.
examples.english: Good day, my name is Ali. Nice to meet you.

head: Reflexivpronomen /ʁeflɛˈksiːfˌpʁoːnoːmən/
gloss: Reflexive pronouns: myself, yourself, himself, etc.
notes: • Reflexive means the subject and object are the same person: Ich wasche mich.
• They follow person and case, not grammatical gender: er/sie/es all use sich.
• Akkusativ forms: mich, dich, sich, sich, sich, uns, euch, sich, sich.
• Dativ forms: mir, dir, sich, sich, sich, uns, euch, sich, sich.
• Use accusative when the reflexive pronoun is the direct object; use dative when another accusative object is present: Ich wasche mich vs. Ich wasche mir die Hände.

examples.english: You all introduce yourselves. [euch; accusative reflexive]
I wash my hands. [mir; dative reflexive]
You buy yourself a book. [dir; dative reflexive]
She buys herself a coffee. [sich; dative reflexive]
It washes its hands. [sich; dative reflexive]

examples.german: Wir stellen uns eine Reise vor.
examples.english: We imagine a trip. [uns; dative reflexive]
You all buy yourselves tickets. [euch; dative reflexive]
They buy themselves tickets. [sich; dative reflexive]

examples.german: Stellen Sie sich eine Stadt vor.
examples.english: Imagine a city. [sich; formal dative reflexive]

head: Personalpronomen /pɛʁzoˈnaːlˌpʁoːnoːmən/
gloss: Personal pronouns: ich, mich, mir; du, dich, dir; etc.
notes: • Pronouns replace nouns: der Mann → er; die Frau → sie; das Kind → es.
• Nominativ marks the subject: ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr, sie.
• Akkusativ marks the direct object: mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie.
• Dativ marks the indirect object or object after dative verbs/prepositions: mir, dir, ihm, ihr, ihm, uns, euch, ihnen.
• Reflexive pronouns can look the same as personal pronouns, but the subject and object are the same person: Ich liebe dich vs. Du liebst dich.

examples.english: I help it. [ihm; dative for a neuter noun]
They come from Germany. [sie; nominative plural]

head: sich vorstellen /zɪç ˈfoːɐ̯ˌʃtɛlən/
gloss: To introduce oneself; to imagine something.
notes: • Separable reflexive verb: vor moves to the end (Ich stelle mich vor.).
• Introduce oneself uses accusative reflexive pronouns: mich, dich, sich, uns, euch, sich.
• Imagine something uses dative reflexive pronouns with an object: mir, dir, sich, uns, euch, sich.

examples.german: Wir stellen uns im Kurs vor.
Stellen Sie sich eine Stadt vor.

head: begrüßen /bəˈɡʁyːsn̩/
gloss: To greet; say hello to someone.
notes: • Takes an accusative object: Begrüßen Sie Ihren Partner / Ihre Partnerin.

examples.english: Greet your partner. [male partner; formal instruction]
Greet your partner. [female partner; formal instruction]
Your partner is here. [male partner; nominative]

head: Wie heißt ...? /viː haɪ̯st/
notes: • W-question pattern: Wie is position 1, the finite verb heißt is position 2.

examples.german: Wie heißt Ihr Land in Ihrer Sprache?
examples.english: What is your country called in your language? [formal]
What is that called in German?

head: die Sprache /ˈʃpʁaːxə/
notes: • Feminine noun: die Sprache; after static in, use dative: in der Sprache / in Ihrer Sprache.

examples.german: In Ihrer Sprache heißt das anders.
examples.english: In your language, that is called something different. [formal]
I am learning the German language.

head: Ihr- /iːɐ̯/
notes: • Capitalized Ihr- means formal your. Endings follow ein-words: Ihr Land, Ihre Sprache, in Ihrer Sprache.

examples.english: I am learning your language. [formal; accusative]
I speak in your language. [formal; dative]

head: fliegen /ˈfliːɡən/
gloss: To fly; travel by plane (+ nach … / zu …).

head: reisen /ˈʁaɪ̯zn̩/
gloss: To travel; go on a trip (+ nach … without article; + in die … when an article appears).

head: fahren /ˈfaːʁən/
gloss: To drive; ride; go by transport (+ nach … for many destinations).
notes: • Strong verb: er fährt; Präteritum fuhr — expect this in texts and exams.

examples.english: We're going to Munich by car / bus / train.
Where are you going? [formal, destination ask]

head: gehen /ˈɡeːən/
gloss: To go; walk (+ zu / nach / + place; also set phrases).
notes: • Irregular present: ich gehe, du gehst, er geht; for whole countries people often prefer fahren/reisen/fliegen.

head: aber /ˈaːbɐ/
gloss: but; however — introduces a contrast or exception
notes: • Coordinating conjunction: word order in the following clause is unchanged (verb stays in position 2).

examples.german: Ich komme aus Deutschland, aber er kommt aus der Schweiz.
examples.english: I come from Germany, but he comes from Switzerland.

examples.german: Die meisten Länder haben keinen Artikel — aber die Schweiz, die Türkei und die Niederlande schon.
examples.english: Most countries have no article — but Switzerland, Turkey, and the Netherlands do.

head: auffallen /ˈaʊ̯fˌfalən/
gloss: To stand out; be noticeable / catch the eye.
notes: • Separable: auf sits at the end of the clause (Was fällt auf?).

examples.english: What do you notice? / What stands out? [reflection question]
The photo stands out; it catches your eye.

head: markieren /maʁˈkiːʁən/
gloss: To mark; highlight verbs or phrases in the text (+ acc.).

examples.german: Markieren Sie den Infinitiv im Text.
examples.english: Mark the infinitive in the passage [instruction].

head: heißen /ˈhaɪ̯sn̩/
gloss: To be called; one’s name is (3rd sg. er/sie/es heißt).
notes: • OCR often miswrites heißen as heisen; spelling is heißen (ß after long diphthong).

head: die W-Frage /ˈveːˌfraːɡə/
gloss: WH-question: starts with Wer, Was, Wo, wie, Wann …
notes: • The question word fills slot 1; the finite verb is still second (verb-second), then the rest.

head: die Antwort /ˈantˌvɔʁt/
gloss: Answer; reply (to a question or task).

examples.german: Schreiben Sie die Antwort in den Satz.
examples.english: Write the answer into the sentence.

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table [class instruction].

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table.

examples.german: Schreiben Sie das in die Tabelle.

head: die Verbform /ˈfɔʁm/
gloss: Verb form (inflected shape of a lemma).
notes: • Misread Verbendung: the word is Verbformen.

examples.german: Sammeln Sie die Verbformen in die Tabelle.
examples.english: Collect the verb forms into the table.

examples.german: Das ist die Übersicht auf einen Blick.
examples.english: That's the overview at a glance.

head: das Verb /vɛʁp/
gloss: Verb (word class for actions and states).

examples.german: Und ich bin Frau Lange, die Sekretärin.
examples.english: And I am Ms. Lange, the secretary.

head: die Frau /fraʊ̯/
gloss: Ms. or Mrs. before a surname.

examples.german: Und ich bin Frau Lange, die Sekretärin.
examples.english: And I am Ms. Lange, the secretary.

head: aus /aʊ̯s/
gloss: From; out of (+ dative for origin).

head: Grüß Gott! /ˈɡʁʏs ˈɡɔt/
gloss: Polite daytime hello—typical in Austria and southern Germany.
notes: • Elsewhere Guten Tag is often the default; Hallo is informal.

examples.german: Lesen Sie das Gespräch und ergänzen Sie.
examples.english: Read the dialogue [aloud/on the page], then finish / fill it in.

examples.german: Lesen Sie die Überschrift zu Beginn der Übung.
examples.english: Read the headline at the start of the exercise.
Two children listen to the dialogue.

examples.german: Das Bild passt zu diesem Text.
examples.english: The picture goes with / fits this text passage.
The second photo does not fit / is wrong.
The photo is sharp / in focus.

head: ankreuzen /ˈankʁɔʏtsən/
notes: • The prefix an- moves to the end of the clause: Kreuzen Sie die richtige Lösung an.
• Participle angekreuzt (perfect often: habe … angekreuzt).

examples.german: Kreuzen Sie die richtige Lösung an.
examples.english: Please tick the correct answer / solution.
I listen to the radio in the mornings.

examples.german: Hören Sie bitte noch einmal das Gespräch.
examples.english: Please listen to the dialogue one more time.

head: neu /nɔʏ/
gloss: New (opposite alt); before a noun, add an ending.

examples.english: A new car is parked there.

head: sein /zaɪn/
notes: • Complement after sein is usually nominative. Present: ich bin · du bist · ist · wir/sie/Sie sind.

examples.german: Und ich bin Frau Lange, die Sekretärin.
examples.english: And I am Ms. Lange, the secretary.
You all are on a team.

head: der Praktikant /pʁaktiˈkant/
gloss: Intern; Praktikantin is the feminine form.

examples.german: Ich mache ein Praktikum in Deutschland.
examples.english: I am doing an internship in Germany.

examples.german: Er ist Praktikant bei dieser Firma.
examples.english: He is an intern at this company.

head: Guten Tag! /ˈɡuːtən ˈtaːk/
notes: • Compare Guten Tag (masculine) vs Gute Nacht (feminine).

examples.german: Guten Tag, wie geht es Ihnen?
